exercises.title: Implement a simple interface
exercises.scenario: Practice the implements keyword and interface method override.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to make Robot implement Greetable.
hints: implements keyword (not extends) for interfaces
@Override documents that you're implementing the interface method
Greetable g = new Robot(...) — interface as reference type

exercises.title: Interface default method
exercises.scenario: Default methods come free with the interface.
hints: City implements getName() (abstract)
describe() is a default method — City gets it for free
describe() calls getName() — polymorphic, runs City's version

exercises.title: Implement Printable interface
exercises.instructions:
Create interface `Printable` with `void print()`. Create class `Invoice` with amount (double) that implements Printable — printing 'Invoice: ₹amount'.

In main, use Printable reference.

Expected output:
Invoice: ₹5299.0
exercises.starter_code: // Define Printable interface and Invoice class
hints: interface keyword, method with no body
Invoice implements Printable — must implement print()
Printable p = new Invoice(...) — interface as reference type

exercises.title: Implement Comparable to sort strings by length
exercises.instructions:
Create class `Word` with private String value. Implement `Comparable<Word>` — natural order is by string LENGTH (shorter first).

Create 4 Words, sort with Arrays.sort, print each.

Expected output:
hi
java
world
programming
hints: implements Comparable<Word>
compareTo: compare lengths using Integer.compare(this.value.length(), other.value.length())
Arrays.sort uses compareTo automatically
toString returns value for easy printing

exercises.title: Fix double subtraction in compareTo
exercises.instructions:
This sorting gives wrong results for some price values. Fix the compareTo.

Expected output (sorted ascending):
299.99
599.0
1999.0

bugs.issue: (int)(this.price - other.price) truncates to int — 299.99 - 599.0 = -299.01 → -299, but 0.3 - 0.1 could give 0 due to precision.

hints: Subtracting doubles and casting to int truncates the decimal
Double.compare handles all edge cases correctly
Returns -1, 0, or 1 — exactly what compareTo needs

exercises.title: Multiple interface implementation
exercises.scenario: A class can implement many interfaces.
exercises.instructions:
Create:
- Interface `Flyable`: `void fly()`
- Interface `Swimmable`: `void swim()`
- Class `Duck` implementing BOTH. fly(): 'Duck flies', swim(): 'Duck swims'.

In main, use both interface types.

Expected output:
Duck flies
Duck swims
exercises.starter_code:
// Define Flyable, Swimmable interfaces
// Duck implements both
hints: class Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable — comma-separated
Must implement all methods from BOTH interfaces
Can use either interface type as a reference

exercises.title: Interface with default method
exercises.scenario: Default method provides free behavior based on abstract method.
exercises.instructions:
Create interface `Scorable`:
- Abstract: `int getScore()`
- Default: `String grade()` — returns 'A' if ≥90, 'B' if ≥80, 'C' if ≥70, 'D' otherwise
- Default: `void report()` prints 'Score: X, Grade: Y'

Create `ExamResult` implementing Scorable with a score field.

Test 3 scores.

Expected output:
Score: 95, Grade: A
Score: 82, Grade: B
Score: 65, Grade: D
exercises.starter_code: // Interface Scorable with abstract getScore() and default grade()/report()

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Score: 95, Grade: A
Score: 82, Grade: B
Score: 65, Grade: D

hints: grade() is default — calls abstract getScore() polymorphically
report() is default — calls both grade() and getScore()
ExamResult only needs to implement getScore()
Scorable[] holds ExamResult objects — polymorphism

exercises.title: Sort employees by salary using Comparable
exercises.scenario: Real domain — Employee sorted by salary descending.
exercises.instructions:
Create `Employee` implementing `Comparable<Employee>` — sorted by salary DESCENDING (highest first).

Fields: name, salary (double). Sort and print name + salary.

Expected output:
Aisha: ₹95000.0
Raj: ₹85000.0
Priya: ₹72000.0
Vikram: ₹68000.0
hints: Descending: return Double.compare(other.salary, this.salary) — arguments reversed
Normal ascending: Double.compare(this.salary, other.salary)
Arrays.sort uses compareTo

exercises.title: Three interfaces on one class
exercises.scenario: Full multi-role class — Comparable, Printable, Serializable-style.
exercises.instructions:
Create:
- Interface `Summarizable`: `String summary()` abstract, default `void printSummary()` calling summary()
- Interface `Scorable`: `int getScore()` abstract

Create `Student` implementing `Comparable<Student>` (by score descending), `Summarizable`, AND `Scorable`.

Fields: name, score (int). Rank them and print summaries.

Expected output:
Aisha: 92
Raj: 87
Priya: 78
hints: Three interfaces after implements, comma-separated
Implement all abstract methods from all three
printSummary() is a default — Student gets it free
Descending score: Integer.compare(other.score, this.score)

exercises.title: Interface as polymorphic type
exercises.scenario: Trace which methods are visible through an interface reference.
hints: f.fly() works — fly() is in Flyable
f.land() would compile error — land() not in Flyable
instanceof Plane p pattern matching — casts and calls land()
Pattern: downcast only when you need specific behavior

exercises.title: Interface extending interface
exercises.instructions:
Create:
- Interface `Readable`: `String read()`
- Interface `Writable`: `void write(String data)`
- Interface `ReadWritable extends Readable, Writable`: no extra methods
- Class `Buffer` implementing `ReadWritable`: stores data internally

In main, use ReadWritable reference to write then read.

Expected output:
Hello CodeQuest
exercises.starter_code:
// Interfaces: Readable, Writable, ReadWritable
// Buffer implements ReadWritable
hints: ReadWritable extends Readable, Writable — both, comma-separated
Buffer must implement read() AND write() from both parent interfaces
ReadWritable as reference type exposes all methods from both parents

exercises.title: Polymorphic method accepting interface type
exercises.scenario: Program to interface — method works for any implementor.
exercises.instructions:
Define interface `Discountable`: `double getPrice()`, `double getDiscountPercent()`.
Default method: `double discountedPrice()` = price * (1 - discountPercent/100).

Create `OnlineProduct` and `StoreProduct` implementing Discountable.

Write static method `printBestDeal(Discountable[] items)` that finds and prints the item with LOWEST discounted price.

Expected output:
Best deal: USB Hub at ₹374.25
hints: discountedPrice() is default — both classes get it free
printBestDeal accepts Discountable[] — works for any implementor
499 × 0.75 = 374.25 — lowest discounted price
Program to interface: method knows nothing about OnlineProduct or StoreProduct

exercises.title: Leaderboard with Comparable
exercises.scenario: Complete gaming leaderboard using natural ordering.
exercises.instructions:
Create `GameEntry` implementing `Comparable<GameEntry>`:
- Fields: playerName (String), score (int), level (int)
- Natural order: DESCENDING score. Tie-break: higher LEVEL first.
- toString: '#N playerName — score pts (lvl L)' (N is rank after sorting)

Sort an array of 5 entries and print with rank.

Expected output:
#1 Aisha — 9500 pts (lvl 8)
#2 Raj — 9500 pts (lvl 6)
#3 Priya — 8200 pts (lvl 7)
#4 Vikram — 7800 pts (lvl 5)
#5 Anita — 7800 pts (lvl 4)

test_cases.expected_stdout:
#1 Aisha — 9500 pts (lvl 8)
#2 Raj — 9500 pts (lvl 6)
#3 Priya — 8200 pts (lvl 7)
#4 Vikram — 7800 pts (lvl 5)
#5 Anita — 7800 pts (lvl 4)

hints: compareTo: first compare scores descending
If scores equal (cmp == 0), compare levels descending as tie-break
Integer.compare(other.score, this.score) = descending
Aisha and Raj both score 9500; Aisha's level 8 > Raj's 6 → Aisha first

exercises.title: Pluggable validation framework
exercises.scenario: Interfaces enabling pluggable behavior — strategy pattern preview.
exercises.instructions:
Create:
- Interface `Validator<T>`: `boolean validate(T value)`, `String errorMessage()`
- Default method: `void check(T value)` — prints error if invalid

Create:
- `LengthValidator implements Validator<String>`: validates string is at least minLength chars
- `RangeValidator implements Validator<Integer>`: validates integer is in [min, max]

Run multiple validators on inputs.

Expected output:
Validating 'hi': [ERROR] String must be at least 5 characters
Validating 'hello': OK
Validating 3: [ERROR] Value must be between 10 and 100
Validating 50: OK
exercises.starter_code:
// Interface Validator<T> with check() default method
// LengthValidator and RangeValidator implementations

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Validating 'hi': [ERROR] String must be at least 5 characters
Validating 'hello': OK
Validating '3': [ERROR] Value must be between 10 and 100
Validating '50': OK

hints: Generic interface Validator<T> — works for String and Integer
check() is default — calls abstract validate() and errorMessage()
LengthValidator: validate checks length, errorMessage explains the rule
This is the Strategy Pattern — swappable validation logic

exercises.title: Complete sortable product catalog
exercises.scenario: Everything together — Comparable, multiple interfaces, polymorphism.
exercises.instructions:
Build a product catalog with:
1. Interface `Categorizable`: `String category()`, default `boolean isElectronics()` → category().equals('Electronics')
2. Interface `Discountable`: `double getPrice()`, `double getDiscount()`, default `double finalPrice()` → price*(1-discount/100)
3. `Product` implementing `Comparable<Product>`, `Categorizable`, `Discountable`:
   - Fields: name, category, price, discount
   - Comparable: ascending finalPrice()
   - All interface methods implemented
4. Static `printElectronics(Categorizable[] items)` — filters and prints only electronics

Expected output:
--- Sorted by final price ---
USB Hub: ₹374.25 (Electronics)
Mouse: ₹539.10 (Electronics)
Java Book: ₹509.15 (Books)
Keyboard: ₹1699.15 (Electronics)

--- Electronics only ---
USB Hub
Mouse
Keyboard

test_cases.expected_stdout:
--- Sorted by final price ---
USB Hub: ₹374.25 (Electronics)
Mouse: ₹539.10 (Electronics)
Java Book: ₹509.15 (Books)
Keyboard: ₹1699.15 (Electronics)

--- Electronics only ---
USB Hub
Mouse
Keyboard

hints: Product implements three types — all abstract methods satisfied
compareTo sorts by finalPrice() ascending
printElectronics accepts Categorizable[] — works for any categorizable
Inside printElectronics, cast to Discountable to call getName()
